findings.title: Significant Drop in Search Relevance for Anonymous Users
findings.description: The recent deployment of v2.4.1 has led to a 15% drop in search relevance scores, disproportionately affecting anonymous users who make up approximately 40% of query volume. This segment is experiencing a significant degradation in search result relevance due to the new ranking algorithm, which relies on historical session data unavailable for non-authenticated requests.
assumptions: Anonymous users are a significant portion of the user base.

evidence.excerpt: Search relevance score dropped 15% after deploy v2.4.1.
The incident disproportionately affected anonymous users (~40% of query volume).
The test was not powered for the anonymous-user segment separately.

recommendations: Re-run A/B test scoped to anonymous users with a properly powered sample size.
Patch ranking model for anonymous user fallback path.

findings.title: Increased Support Ticket Volume Due to Irrelevant Results
findings.description: Support ticket volume has increased threefold since the v2.4.1 deployment, primarily due to users reporting irrelevant search results. This indicates a critical pain point where users are unable to find relevant information efficiently, leading to frustration and potential churn.
assumptions: Users expect relevant search results based on their queries.

evidence.excerpt: Users are reporting that top results no longer match their intent.
Support ticket volume increased 3x in the 6 days following the v2.4.1 deployment.
The vast majority of complaints fall into two categories: irrelevant search results and slow search on mobile.

recommendations: Implement immediate fixes to improve search relevance.
Consider a rollback to the previous version if issues persist.

findings.title: Mobile Search Latency Increased Significantly
findings.description: Search latency on mobile web has increased to 890ms, significantly affecting user experience. This increase in latency is likely contributing to the overall dissatisfaction with the search functionality, particularly for mobile users.
assumptions: Users expect fast response times for search queries.

evidence.excerpt: P95 search latency on mobile web has climbed to 890ms.
Mobile app search takes forever to load now.

recommendations: Profile ranking re-ranker for mobile session path to identify optimization opportunities.
Implement mobile-specific caching to reduce latency.

findings.title: Stale Autocomplete Suggestions Leading to User Frustration
findings.description: The autocomplete feature is providing stale suggestions due to a misconfiguration that extended the cache TTL to 7 days. This results in users being directed to out-of-stock products or expired promotions, compounding the relevance issues.
assumptions: Users rely on autocomplete for efficient searching.

evidence.excerpt: Users are seeing suggestions for products that are out of stock or promotions that have ended.
A smaller but growing segment reports broken autocomplete suggestions leading to dead-end pages.

recommendations: Correct the autocomplete cache TTL to 24 hours and validate the fix.
Monitor user feedback on autocomplete suggestions post-fix.

summary: The recent deployment of v2.4.1 has led to significant issues in search relevance, particularly affecting anonymous users and mobile search performance. Increased support ticket volume indicates user frustration with irrelevant results and slow response times. Immediate actions are required to address these pain points, including re-running A/B tests for anonymous users, optimizing mobile search latency, and correcting stale autocomplete suggestions.
